coredata.dc:title: Chitosan-derived carbon aerogel modified with lignin carbon quantum dots for efficient solar evaporation
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Zichen
Li, Dazhen
Wei, Shuxia
Jin, Xin
Zhang, Zheng
Liu, Lin
Ge, Yuanyuan
Li, Zhili
coredata.dc:description: Solar-powered desalination technology offers outstanding benefits in providing clean, safe water and alleviating global freshwater shortages. However, the low evaporation rate, poor salt resistance and high cost limit the development of solar evaporators. Inspired by plant transpiration, a solar evaporator was fabricated from lignin carbon quantum dots (LCQDs) modified chitosan carbon aerogel (C-LCDCA). The introduced LCQDs and low carbonization temperature (280 °C) ensured hydrophilic properties while maintaining the three-dimensional porous structure, as well as improved light absorption and photothermal conversion properties. The evaporation rate of C-LCDCA under one sun was 1.71 kg m−2h−1. Interestingly, C-LCDCA promoted the local crystallization of salt crystals and realized the collection. The evaporation performance of the evaporator can be adapted to different evaporation situations by making simple layer adjustments. The optimal C-LCDCA evaporator (4-layer integrated) surpassed the limit of the interfacial evaporation system, with evaporation rate and efficiency up to 2.32 kg m−2h−1 and 113.9 % respectively under one sun, and with strong salt-rejecting (no obvious salt crystallization in 20 wt% brine for 8 h) and excellent self-cleaning ability (0.3 g NaCl re-dissolved back to 15 wt% brine in 15 min). With superior evaporation performance, the condensate obtained by the evaporator in simulated seawater evaporation experiments met the World Health Organization’s drinking water standards, and enabled effective treatment of industrial wastewater containing heavy metal ions (Cr3+, Fe3+, Pb2+, and Ni2+), nanoplastics (500 and 50 nm), and dyes (Rhodamine B, Methyl Orange, and Malachite green). These findings provide new ideas for the preparation of efficient, salt-rejecting, self-cleaning, and environmentally friendly solar evaporators and advance the practical application of sustainable water purification technologies.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Lignin carbon quantum dots
Chitosan carbon aerogel
Solar thermal conversion
Steam generation
Water desalination and purification

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of lignin carbon quantum dots (LCQDs)
Synthesis of LCQDs modified chitosan carbon aerogel (C-LCDCA)
Efficient evaporation, salt-rejecting, and self-cleaning properties
Purification of various water sources during evaporation

National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21978057 ), Guangxi Natural Science Foundation of China (No. AD21220012 ), 100-Talent Program of Guangxi, China, Innovation Project of Guangxi Graduate Education ( YCSW2023089 ), and the Students Innovation and Entrepreneurship Training Program of Guangxi University ( S202210593142 ), China.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) The preparation process of LCQDs; (b) TEM images and particle size distribution (inset) of LCQDs; (c) HRTEM image of LCQDs; (d-g) EDS elemental mapping images of LCQDs.
The images of the sample and the SEM images of the cross-section after brittle fracture by liquid nitrogen: (a-d) CA, (e-h) LCDCA, (i-l) C-CA, and (m-p) C-LCDCA.

(a) FTIR and (b) XRD of chitosan power, CA, LCDCA, C-CA, and C-LCDCA; (c) C1s spectra of CA, LCDCA, C-CA, and C-LCDCA; (d-e) Water contact angle variation over time for C-CA and C-LCDCA; (f) Light absorption (right axis) of CA, LCDCA, C-CA, and C-LCDCA, and solar spectral irradiance (left axis); (g) The temperature changes of the CA, LCDCA, C-CA, and C-LCDCA evaporation interfaces under one sun; (h) The infrared thermal imaging of C-CA and C-LCDCA evaporators with 30 min under one sun.

(a) The schematic diagram of the solar-powered water evaporation device; (b) The mass change of water with time and (c) evaporation rates and efficiency of pure water, CA, LCDCA, C-CA, and C-LCDCA; (d) The evaporation rates of C-LCDCAN (N from 0 to 6); (e) The evaporative performance of C-LCDCA for 12 cycles of evaporation (Insets: the mass change of water with time in the 1 and 12 cycles); (f) The evaporation performance of C-LCDCA in different pH solutions; (g) The salt resistance of C-LCDCA in solutions with different salt concentrations (3.5 wt% < WNaCl < 15 wt%) by continuous evaporation for 8 h (precipitation of salt crystals); (h) The evaporation performance of C-LCDCA4i in solutions with different salt concentrations; (i) The salt resistance of C-LCDCA4i in solutions with different salt concentrations (15 wt% < WNaCl < 25 wt%) by continuous evaporation for 8 h (precipitation of salt crystals).
The mechanism of efficient evaporation, salt resistance, and self-cleaning performance of C-LCDCA.

(a) The effect of evaporation processes on four ions (Na+, Mg2+, K+, and Ca2+) in simulated seawater; (b) The effect of evaporation process on dye wastewater (RhB, MO, and MG) and nanoplastics wastewater (500 and 50 nm); (c) The effect of evaporation process on wastewater containing heavy metal ions (Cr3+, Fe3+, Pb2+, and Ni2+); (d) The mass change with time under natural outdoor sunlight for C-LCDCA4i.

head.ce:title: Chitosan-derived carbon aerogel modified with lignin carbon quantum dots for efficient solar evaporation

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Guangxi University, 100 Daxuedong Road, Nanning 530004, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Guangxi University, 100 Daxuedong Road, Nanning 530004, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work and should be considered co-first authors.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Solar-powered desalination technology offers outstanding benefits in providing clean, safe water and alleviating global freshwater shortages. However, the low evaporation rate, poor salt resistance and high cost limit the development of solar evaporators. Inspired by plant transpiration, a solar evaporator was fabricated from lignin carbon quantum dots (LCQDs) modified chitosan carbon aerogel (C-LCDCA). The introduced LCQDs and low carbonization temperature (280 °C) ensured hydrophilic properties while maintaining the three-dimensional porous structure, as well as improved light absorption and photothermal conversion properties. The evaporation rate of C-LCDCA under one sun was 1.71 kg m−2h−1. Interestingly, C-LCDCA promoted the local crystallization of salt crystals and realized the collection. The evaporation performance of the evaporator can be adapted to different evaporation situations by making simple layer adjustments. The optimal C-LCDCA evaporator (4-layer integrated) surpassed the limit of the interfacial evaporation system, with evaporation rate and efficiency up to 2.32 kg m−2h−1 and 113.9 % respectively under one sun, and with strong salt-rejecting (no obvious salt crystallization in 20 wt% brine for 8 h) and excellent self-cleaning ability (0.3 g NaCl re-dissolved back to 15 wt% brine in 15 min). With superior evaporation performance, the condensate obtained by the evaporator in simulated seawater evaporation experiments met the World Health Organization’s drinking water standards, and enabled effective treatment of industrial wastewater containing heavy metal ions (Cr3+, Fe3+, Pb2+, and Ni2+), nanoplastics (500 and 50 nm), and dyes (Rhodamine B, Methyl Orange, and Malachite green). These findings provide new ideas for the preparation of efficient, salt-rejecting, self-cleaning, and environmentally friendly solar evaporators and advance the practical application of sustainable water purification technologies.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Water scarcity is gradually becoming a worldwide challenge with modern industrial development and increasing population [1,2]. Solar seawater evaporators convert solar energy into heat for water evaporation, alleviating the global water crisis with a minimal carbon footprint [3]. Compared to traditional desalination technologies such as multi-effect distillation [4], reverse osmosis [5] and electrodialysis [6], desalination technology powered by clean solar energy without the need for complex equipment and facilities, consumption of large amounts of fossil fuels and electricity resources, offers the advantages of simple process, low cost, and environmental friendliness. The solar interface system positions photothermal conversion and water evaporation at the gas–liquid interface, selectively heating the portion to be evaporated instead of the complete bulk water. This approach differs from bottom heating [7] and volumetric [8] systems, to achieve efficient solar-steam conversion performance of the evaporator [7,9–11]. The water evaporation performance of solar interface evaporators is mainly influenced by three aspects: the light absorption capacity of the photothermal material, the thermal management of the evaporation system, and the water supply [12,13].

Bio-based materials are one of the most promising solar evaporation materials for practical applications due to their excellent natural structure, high stability, low cost, and wide range of sources compared to traditional light-absorbing materials including metallic materials [14], semiconductor materials [15] and carbon-based materials [16,17]. Chitosan is produced through the deacetylation of chitin, the second largest natural polymer in the world, mainly from the shells of shrimps, crabs, and insects. It is a renewable resource with wide distribution in nature and can be composed of the solar evaporator [18,19]. Chitosan aerogel exists as a matrix material in conventional chitosan aerogel solar evaporators, providing a carrier and water supply for the photothermal conversion material [12,20]. The chitosan aerogels modified with TiN powder (1.49 kg m−2h−1), MnO2 powder (1.52 kg m−2h−1), graphite powder (1.47 kg m−2h−1), multi-walled carbon nanotube powder (1.70 kg m−2h−1), graphene oxide dispersion (1.58 kg m−2h−1), commercial carbon ink (1.54 kg m−2h−1) and plant fiber (1.38 kg m−2h−1) still face the challenges, such as lower evaporation rates, higher costs, and poor application prospects [12,19]. The novel photothermal conversion materials, which have the potential for practical use in solar seawater evaporation, merit further investigation.

Quantum dot is a zero-dimensional nanomaterial [21,22]. Carbon quantum dots are different from traditional semiconductor quantum dots and possess the merits of environmental friendliness, simple process, and low cost [22–24]. Lignin, as the waste from the paper industry, features a rich aromatic structure and oxygen-containing functional groups and serves as a promising biomass precursor material for the preparation of carbon quantum dots [25–27]. Lignin carbon quantum dots can be used as novel photothermal materials to modify chitosan aerogels for the preparation of solar seawater evaporators. In addition, due to the extremely small particle size (less than 10 nm) of lignin carbon quantum dots, the modification and doping process will not damage the structure of the matrix material, ensuring the stability of the material. The thermal management of the evaporation system and the water supply play an important role in the water evaporation performance of the solar interface evaporator, in addition to the light absorption capacity of the photothermal material [13]. The additional insulation and water supply structure allows for thermal management of the evaporation system while ensuring adequate water supply to further enhance the efficiency of the evaporation system [7,12].

Here, a bionic lignin carbon quantum dot modified chitosan carbon aerogel (C-LCDCA) evaporator was prepared inspired by plants transpiration. The lignin carbon quantum dot modified chitosan aerogel (LCDCA) was prepared by a one-step freeze-drying technique using lignin carbon quantum dots and chitosan gel solution. After low-temperature carbonization (280 °C), C-LCDCA was obtained. While retaining the excellent hydrophilic properties of chitosan aerogel, low-temperature carbonization transformed it into chitosan carbon aerogel, serving not only as a matrix material but also as a light-absorbent material when composing evaporation materials to further improve light-absorbing capabilities. Paper belt water supply and polystyrene foam insulation are used to ensure adequate water supply while avoiding direct contact of evaporation materials with the bulk water, achieving excellent thermal efficiency of the evaporation system. The evaporation rate of the bionic evaporator consisting of a single layer of C-LCDCA was 1.71 kg m−2h−1. Interestingly, C-LCDCA realized the local crystallization of salt crystals and realize the collection. Varying the number of layers of C-LCDCA to explore the effect of height variation on evaporator performance, the best 3D C-LCDCA evaporator (4-layer integrated) surpassed the limits of 2D system interface evaporation, and the evaporation rate and efficiency could reach 2.32 kg m−2h−1 and 113.9 % respectively under one solar illumination. The stability (strong acids and bases), salt tolerance (different salt concentrations), and self-cleaning ability (re-dissolving salt crystals back into the bulk water) of the evaporator were investigated. The evaporator provided superior evaporation performance and the condensate obtained from simulated seawater met the drinking standards of the World Health Organization (WHO). Moreover, the evaporator achieved effective treatment of industrial wastewater containing heavy metal ions (Cr3+, Fe3+, Pb2+, and Ni2+), nanoplastics (500 nm and 50 nm), and dyes (Rhodamine B, Methyl Orange, and Malachite Green), with removal rates close to 100 %. These results indicate that the bionic evaporator based on C-LCDCA offers new opportunities for sustainable desalination and wastewater purification technologies with promising practical applications.

Alkaline lignin was purchased from TCI (Shanghai) Development Co., Ltd. Chitosan (MW = 30000) and epichlorohydrin were purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. L-Cysteine was purchased from Chengdu Cologne Chemical Co., Ltd. Dialysis bags (3500 Da) were purchased from Shanghai yuanye Bio-Technology Co., Ltd. Filter paper was purchased from Fushun Civil Administration Filter Paper Factory. NaCl, KCl, CaCl2, MgCl2·6H2O, Pb(NO3)2, Fe(NO3)3·9H2O, and Ni(NO3)2·6H2O were purchased from Guangdong Xinghua Technology Co., Ltd. Cr(NO3)3·9H2O was purchased from Tianjin Damao Chemical Reagent Factory. Rhodamine B (RhB) and Methyl Orange (MO) were purchased from Tianjin Kemiou Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Malachite green (MG) was purchased from Tianjin Zhiyuan Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Polystyrene microspheres (PS) (50/500 nm) were purchased from Zhongkeleiming Technology Co., Ltd. All reagents used were analytical purity without further purification. The experimental water was deionized water.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of lignin carbon quantum dots (LCQDs)
ce:section.ce:para: LCQDs were prepared according to the process shown in Fig. 1 a. The LCQDs solution was obtained by mixing 0.3 g of alkaline lignin and 0.2 g of cysteine in 30 ml of deionized water, stirring thoroughly for 2 h, and then transferring to a 50 ml hydrothermal reactor and heating in an oven at 180 °C for 12 h. The crude LCQDs solution was dialyzed in a 3500 Da dialysis bag for 72 h at room temperature to obtain the pure LCQDs solution. LCQDs was obtained by freeze-drying the pure LCQDs solution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of LCQDs modified chitosan carbon aerogel (C-LCDCA)
ce:section.ce:para: A certain amount of LCQDs was added to 50 ml of 2 wt% acetic acid solution and stirred continuously for 1 h. Then 1 g of chitosan was added and stirred for 4 h. After adding 1 ml of epichlorohydrin and stirring for 1 h, LCQDs modified chitosan gel solution was obtained by continuing stirring for 1 h at 70 °C in a water bath. A certain amount of gel solution was poured into the mold and frozen for 12 h in a refrigerator and freeze-dried for 24 h to obtain the LCQDs modified chitosan aerogel (LCDCA). C-LCDCA was obtained by heating the LCDCA at 280 °C for 1 h at a heating rate of 5 °C/min under an air atmosphere. Chitosan aerogel (CA) and carbonized chitosan aerogel (C-CA) were prepared according to the same way without the addition of LCQDs.

The surface morphology of CA, LCDCA, C-CA, C-LCDCA, and C-LCDCA4i was observed by scanning electron microscope (SEM, ZEISS Sigma 300, Germany). The LCQDs was observed by transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEOL 2100F, Japan). The UV–Vis absorption spectra of LCQDs and the concentration of NPs (500 and 50 nm) and dyes (Rhodamine B, Methyl Orange, and Malachite Green) in water were observed by an ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) spectrophotometer (UV-1800PC, China). The FTIR spectra of lignin, LCQDs, chitosan power, CA, LCDCA, C-CA, and C-LCDCA with the range of 500–4000 cm−1 were recorded by Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FTIR, Perkin-Elmer, USA). The XRD patterns of LCQDs, chitosan power, CA, LCDCA, C-CA, and C-LCDCA in the range of 5-80° were characterized by an X-ray diffraction analyzer (XRD, MiniFlex600, Japan). The composition and state of elements of LCQDs, CA, LCDCA, C-CA, and C-LCDCA were characterized by an X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS, Thermo Scientific K-Alpha, USA). The contact angles of C-CA and C-LCDCA were measured by a contact angle goniometer (JY-82C, Chengde Dingsheng Testing Equipment Co.). The light absorption of CA, LCDCA, C-CA, and C-LCDCA was characterized by an ultraviolet–visible-near infrared (UV–Vis-NIR) spectrometer (Lambda 950, Perkin-Elmer, USA) with an integrating sphere. The concentrations of Na+, Mg2+, K+, Ca2+ Cr3+, Fe3+, Pb2+, and Ni2+ before and after solar-powered evaporation were measured by inductively coupled plasma atomic emission spectroscopy (ICP-MS, Agilent 720ES, USA).

The solar-powered evaporation experiments were conducted at a simulated radiation of one sun intensity under ambient environment (25 °C, a relative humidity of 50 %). The simulated light source is provided by a 300 W xenon lamp (CEL-S500/350, China) with an AM1.5 filter. The mass change of water during the evaporation process was measured using an electronic balance (BS224S, Germany). The aerogel samples were floated on the water surface through polystyrene foam, with water supply through a paper belt (Fig. S3), which allowed for an adequate supply of water needed for evaporation, as shown in the electronic supplementary information (ESI). The temperature of the surface of the evaporator was measured by an infrared camera (TP8S, China). The evaporation performance of an evaporator was evaluated by evaporation rate and efficiency, which were calculated as shown in the ESI.

As shown in Fig. 1a, LCQDs were synthesized from lignin and L-cysteine by a one-step hydrothermal reaction. LCQDs possessed excellent water solubility and stability, and 400 mg L−1 of the solution did not sediment over several months (Fig. S2). The UV–Vis absorption spectra were used to evaluate the optical properties of the prepared LCQDs (Fig. S4), with an obvious UV absorption peak at 235 nm, which was caused by the π-π* jump of the aromatic structure [28]. The TEM image of the LCQDs is presented in Fig. 1b and c. LCQDs were dispersed uniformly in the aqueous solution without any agglomeration. The particle size distribution was obtained by measuring the particle size of 300 LCQDs. The particle size distribution of LCQDs was in the range of 1.0–5.0 nm with an average size of 1.9 nm. The corresponding HRTEM image revealed that the LCQDs had an ordered lattice stripe structure with a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm, which belonged to the (100) plane of ideal graphitic carbon, indicating that the LCQDs formed a graphite-like carbon core structure during hydrothermal carbonization [29–31].

Fig. 1d-g presented the EDS analysis of LCQDs, N and S elements were introduced through L-cysteine, and the uniform distribution of the elements (C, O, N, and S). The XRD pattern of LCQDs showed a weaker diffraction peak at 2θ = 42.3° (Fig. S5), corresponding to the (100) plane of graphitic carbon (d100 = 0.21 nm) (PDF#41-1487)[32,33]. Moreover, a stronger diffraction peak was found at 2θ = 23.7°, close to the (002) diffraction plane of graphitic carbon [34]. The functional groups and chemical composition in LCQDs were examined by FTIR (Fig. S6) and XPS (Figs. S7 and S8). The FTIR image showed that the synthesized LCQDs retained the basic structure of alkali lignin. The broad peak at 3510 cm−1 corresponding to the stretching vibration of −OH, the absorption peak at 3200 cm−1 attributed to the symmetric stretching vibration of −NH2, and the absorption peaks at 2936 and 2837 cm−1 attributed to the stretching vibration of CH. The three absorption peaks at 1600, 1510, and 1421 cm−1 were attributed to the CC stretching vibration of the aromatic benzene ring, which is the characteristic peak of the lignin benzene ring. The absorption peaks at 1700, 1457, 1365, and 1081 cm−1 are attributed to the CO, CO, CN, and CS stretching vibrations, respectively. The XPS survey of LCQDs exhibited four characteristic peaks at 285.21, 533.00, 400.49, and 164.03 eV, which were attributed to C1s (80.32 %), O1s (18.17 %), N1s (0.59 %) and S2p (0.77 %), respectively. The appearance of N1s and S2p characteristic peaks further demonstrated the successful N, S co-doping of alkaline lignin by L-cysteine. FTIR and XPS spectra analysis revealed that LCQDs possess easily functionalized surface active sites, probably due to the rich aromatic structure and oxygen-containing functional groups of lignin, and the N, S co-doping of L-cysteine [34,35]. The above analysis revealed that LCQDs were successfully prepared from alkaline lignin by a simple and green one-step hydrothermal method.

C-LCDCA was prepared according to the process in Fig. S9. Fig. 2 shows the photos of the aerogels and the SEM images of the cross-section of CA, LCDCA, C-CA, and C-LCDCA after brittle fracture by liquid nitrogen. The appearance of CA was white, with an interpenetrating three-dimensional mesh-like porous structure, consisting of micron pores (the mean pore size was 123.92 μm) with relatively uniform structure and size (Fig. 2a–d and Fig. S10a). The LCDCA showed a brown appearance due to the introduction of LCQDs and had a three-dimensional mesh-like porous structure and pore size (the mean pore size was 121.41 μm) comparable to that of CA, indicating the doping of LCQDs did not affect the space structure of the chitosan aerogel (Fig. 2e–h and Fig. S10b). C-CA and C-LCDCA both showed a black appearance after carbonization (Fig. 2i and m). Increased number of C-CA and C-LCDCA pores and decreased pore wall thickness due to the loss of small molecules such as water during carbonization. The LCQDs containing oxygen groups which interacted with the carboxyl, amino, and hydroxyl groups on chitosan through hydrogen bonding promoted the polymerization and cross-linking of chitosan chains and deepened the carbonization of chitosan aerogels during the carbonization process [36].

The pore sizes of C-CA and C-LCDCA were 88.27 and 61.43 µm, respectively (Fig. S10c and d). Compared with C-CA, the decreased pore size, increased pore number, and thinner pore wall of C-LCDCA further indicated that the introduction of LCQD enhanced the carbonation (Fig. 2i–p). Nitrogen adsorption–desorption isotherms were further utilized to study the porous structure of chitosan aerogels (Fig. S12a and b). The N2 adsorption–desorption isotherms of CA, LCDCA, C-CA, and C-LCDCA were all type IV isotherms with H3-type hysteresis loops, which indicated the presence of both micropores and mesopores in the aerogels, and the pores showed an overall slit shape [20,36,37]. The pore sizes of CA, LCDCA, C-CA, and C-LCDCA were 5.25, 6.17, 4.93, and 5.37 nm (Fig. S12 and Table S2), indicating that the doping of LCQD also had essentially no effect on the microscopic nanopore structure of chitosan aerogel materials. In addition, similar to the change in micron pore size before and after carbonization of chitosan aerogels observed by SEM, a decrease in nanopore size also occurred after carbonization.

The crystal structure and surface chemical composition of chitosan powder, CA, LCDCA, C-CA, and C-LCDCA were evaluated by XRD, FT-IR, and XPS. Fig. 3 b shows the XRD spectra of the samples. The crystallinity of the chitosan aerogel decreased compared to that of the original chitosan powder [38]. The XRD patterns of LCDCA did not significantly change, indicating that the introduction of LCQDs had less effect on the structure of chitosan aerogels. Moreover, the diffraction pattern of the carbonized sample had only one broad peak, indicating the amorphous character [12,39]. The FTIR spectra of the samples are illustrated in Fig. 3a and Fig. S13. The distinct peaks of 3430, 3380, 2871, 1151, and 1078 cm−1 were attributed to the stretching vibrations of OH, NH, CH, COC, and CO [39,40]. The absorption peak at 1378 cm−1 was due to the bending vibration of −CH2; the peaks at 1600 and 890 cm−1 were the in-plane bending vibration of NH and the out-of-plane bending vibration of NH, respectively [41]. The main characteristic peaks associated with chitosan and LCQDs were observed in the FTIR spectra of all chitosan aerogels (Fig. S13). The absorption peak at 2871 and 1151 cm−1 of the carbonized chitosan aerogel became weaker owing to the conversion of CH and COC to small molecules such as H2O and CO2 through the carbonization process of chitosan aerogel [39,42].

Such results were further confirmed by the XPS survey (Fig. S14) and high-resolution spectra of C1s and O1s of CA, LCDCA, C-CA, and C-LCDCA (Fig. 3c and Fig. S15). The relative content of oxygen and nitrogen in LCDCA increased from 30.80 % and 6.36 % to 41.13 % and 7.66 %, respectively, as compared to CA, due to the introduction of LCQD into chitosan aerogel (Fig. S14 and Table S3). The relative carbon content increased from 62.84 % to 71.50 % with the loss of small molecules, such as water, during the carbonization of CA to C-CA, further corroborating the decrease in pore size and the thinner pore walls in the SEM images. The successful introduction of LCQDs was further confirmed by the increased relative contents of CC/CC and CO in LCDCA compared with the XPS spectra of C1s and O1s of CA (Tables S4 and S5). The relative contents of CC/CC increased after the carbonization of CA and LCDCA into C-CA and C-LCDCA, respectively, while the relative contents of CO underwent a decrease as the increase of CO. The XPS spectra analysis of the samples further demonstrated the successful preparation of LCQDs modified chitosan aerogel. The successful introduction of LCQDs in chitosan aerogels to enhance carbonization during the carbonization process further revealed that C-LCDCA has more pores with thinner pore walls compared to C-CA. Moreover, the introduction of more oxygen-containing functional groups ensures that C-LCDCA features good hydrophilicity.

Excellent hydrophilicity is an important factor in ensuring the high evaporation performance of evaporation materials [43,44]. As shown in Fig. 3d and e, the water droplets fell and penetrated the surface of C-CA and C-LCDCA quickly with good wetting performance. The time required for complete immersion of a water droplet in C-LCDCA was reduced by half compared to C-CA. The introduction of LCQDs further enhanced the wetting performance of C-LCDCA. The C-LCDCA offered good wetting performance to ensure adequate water supply and provide the basis for improved salt-rejecting and self-cleaning ability of the evaporation material [20,45].

The light absorption properties of the photothermal material affects the evaporation efficiency of the solar evaporator [7,46]. The light absorption ability of CA, LCDCA, C-CA, and C-LCDCA in the range of 300–2500 nm was measured by UV–Vis-NIR absorption spectroscopy (Fig. 3f). The average light absorbance of the samples in the UV (300–400 nm), visible (400–750 nm), NIR (750–2500 nm) and full region (300–2500 nm) is shown in Table S6. The average light absorbance in the full region (300–2500 nm) range was 17.46 % and 49.64 % for CA and LCDCA, respectively, indicating that the introduction of LCQDs can significantly improve the light absorption of chitosan aerogels. The average light absorbance of C-LCDCA (89.84 %) was 1.81 times higher than that of LCDCA (49.64 %) in the full range, indicating that the carbonization process could further enhance the light absorption performance of the photothermal material. Especially in the UV and visible regions, the average light absorption of C-LCDCA is up to 97.28 % and 96.22 %, respectively.

To further investigated the photothermal conversion properties of the materials, an infrared thermal imager was used to measure the temperature changes (Fig. 3g and Fig. S16) and distributions (Fig. 3h and Fig. S17) of dry and wet CA, LCDCA, C-CA, and C-LCDCA, respectively, under one solar intensity. The surface temperature increased from 41.6 °C for dry CA to 56.6 °C for dry LCDCA due to the addition of LCQDs; the surface temperature further increased to 64.4 °C for dry C-CA and 68.0 °C for dry C-LCDCA after low-temperature carbonization after 1 h of irradiation (Fig. S16). The temperature changes in the above dry materials were consistent with the light harvesting ability (Fig. 3f). The surface temperatures of wet aerogels were significantly lower than those of dry aerogels owing to the energy consumption of the water evaporation process. The surface temperatures of wet CA, LCDCA, C-CA, and C-LCDCA were 32.9, 36.8, 37.9, and 39.1 °C, respectively, after 1 h of irradiation, indicating that LCQDs doping and low-temperature carbonization still maintain good photothermal conversion enhancement performance under actual water evaporation conditions (Fig. 3g). Under actual water evaporation conditions, the surface temperature of C-LCDCA reached 36.5 °C within 5 mins, which was 4.1 °C higher than that of C-CA; the surface temperature of C-LCDCA stabilized at about 38.0 °C after 10 mins, which was 5 mins faster than that of C-CA. This showed that C-LCDCA has fast photothermal conversion performance under solar-powered desalination conditions.

The results of the water contact angle test, UV–Vis-NIR absorption spectra, and surface temperature change showed that C-LCDCA has good water transport performance, light absorption performance, and photothermal conversion performance, ensuring that it can serve as an excellent light evaporation material for solar-powered desalination. Furthermore, the heat loss of C-LCDCA during evaporation was calculated (Note S3). The heat loss of radiation, conduction, and convection are 1.23, 4.41, and 2.00 %, respectively. The calculated results showed that the evaporation system effectively reduced convection and heat transfer from the system to the bulk water and environment, improved the thermal utilization of the system, and achieved efficient solar water evaporation.

The water evaporation performance of the solar-powered evaporator was evaluated by the mass change of water over time. The evaporation experiments were carried out under one sun without special emphasis. The aerogel was floated on the water surface by polystyrene foam and supplied with water by paper belts (Fig. 4 a). The water transfer paper belt had good long-term performance and could ensure stable operation of the evaporator (Fig. S26). The study investigated the effect of carbonization temperature on the evaporation ability of C-CA and found that the ideal temperature for carbonization is 280 °C (Fig. S18 to 20 and 25a). Lower carbonization temperatures could ensure a lower degree of carbonization, while higher carbonization temperatures would destroy the structure of the material (Fig. S18 to 20). The microscopic morphology, pore distribution, and average pore size of chitosan aerogels did not change significantly with increasing doping of LCQDs (Figs. S21 and S22). The light-absorbing properties and the elemental sulfur content of chitosan aerogels both increased gradually with the increase in the content of LCQDs (Figs. S23 and S24). The effect of addition of LCQD on the evaporation properties of C-LCDCA was further evaluated and showed that the optimal weight ratio of LCQD to chitosan was 1:15 (Fig. S25b). The evaporation performance saturated with the increase of content, and the excess of LCQDs would weaken the scattering and absorption of light in the pores, resulting in a decrease in the evaporation rate [12,36].

C-LCDCA provided the best evaporation performance with an evaporation rate of 1.71 kg m−2h−1, which was 1.2, 1.8, 2.9, and 5.3 times higher than that of C-CA, LCDCA, CA, and pure water, respectively (Fig. 4b and c). As seen in Table S7, the evaporation performance of C-LCDCA was comparable to biomass-based solar evaporators. Side evaporation is an important factor affecting the evaporation performance of 3D evaporators. Here, the height of the photothermal evaporation layer was controlled by simple changing the number of layers, which in turn varied the side evaporation to meet the different evaporation requirements. The C-LCDCA (D = 19 mm, h = 5 mm) as one layer was stacked vertically along the bottom surface of the cylinder to form evaporators with different numbers of photothermal layers (Fig. S27). The number of layers with N was designated as C-LCDCAN (N from 0 to 6). C-LCDCAN had different heights depending on the number of layers. C-LCDCAN had the same area of light radiation and different side areas in the xenon lamp simulated evaporation experiment.

The experimental results showed that the evaporation rate reached the highest rate of 2.21 kg m−2h−1 when N = 4 (Fig. 4d). The evaporation rate increased with increasing height when N < 4. The hydrophilicity of C-LCDCAN (N < 4) ensured an adequate water supply under highly increased conditions, guaranteeing the efficient occurrence of solar water evaporation from the top of the material. The increased side area ensured the utilization of heat conducted from the top and improved the utilization of energy from the environment. The evaporation rate decreased with increasing height when N > 4. The decrease in the total evaporation rate of the material was caused by a decrease in the top evaporation rate. As more layers were added, the water supply at the top of the material decreased due to gravity. When N > 4, the water supply at the top of the material was no longer adequate for its own evaporation action and a decrease in the evaporation rate occurred. Furthermore, as the number of layers increased, liquid water molecules had to cross more interlayer gaps to reach the surface of the material for evaporation. This process also limited the total number of layers in the material. The above analysis showed that the optimum number of layers in the material was 4.

The C-LCDCA4 synthesized by plastic mold integration was labeled as C-LCDCA4i (D = 19 mm, h = 20 mm). The micromorphology, pore distribution, and average pore size of C-LCDCA4i were similar to those of C-LCDCA, indicating that the spatial structure of the evaporated material before and after integration was not significantly affected (Fig. 2m-p, Figs. S28, and S29). The EDS images indicated that the major elements were uniformly distributed on the surface of the material, further indicating the successful preparation of the C-LCDCA4i evaporated material (Fig. S30). Interestingly, the evaporation rate of C-LCDCA4i increased to 2.32 kg m−2h−1 after integrated synthesis, due to more coherent water transport and heat transfer and utilization (Fig. S31). The evaporation performance of C-LCDCA4i enhanced by lateral evaporation had far exceeded that of conventional biomass evaporators made of natural materials as well as reported chitosan aerogel-based evaporators (Table S7).

Furthermore, the stability and durability of the evaporation materials are important factors in evaluating solar evaporators. The evaporation rate of C-LCDCA remained stable after 12 cycles and no significant decrease occurred in different pH solutions (Fig. 4e and f), indicating that the evaporator has good stability and durability. Salt resistance in salt solution is an important factor when considering the desalination performance of solar evaporators because the concentration of salt solution increases as solar seawater evaporation progresses. C-LCDCA exhibited a high evaporation rate in salt solutions, even in saturated salt solutions (WNaCl = 26.4 wt%) the evaporation rate could still reach 1.18 kg m−2h−1 (Fig. S32). The salt resistance of continuous evaporation for 8 h under one sun is usually considered as a key factor to measure the long-term desalination performance of evaporators, considering the real sunlight duration and intensity.

Salt crystals precipitated on the surface of the solar evaporator will affect the subsequent solar evaporation by affecting the light absorption of the material, blocking the water supply and the capillary pores of the vapor generation. Fig. 4g and Fig. S33 showed the salt crystallization on the surface of the evaporator after 8 h of continuous evaporation of C-LCDCA in salt solutions of different concentrations, respectively. When WNaCl < 5 wt%, no obvious salt crystallization was observed on the surface of C-LCDCA after 8 h of continuous evaporation. When the salt concentration was increased to 10 wt%, salt crystals were precipitated at the edge of C-LCDCA after 4 h of continuous evaporation, and the volume gradually increased with time, but no obvious salt crystals were precipitated on the surface of C-LCDCA during the evaporation process. At WNaCl = 15 wt%, salt crystals precipitated heavily at the edges of C-LCDCA after 8 h of continuous evaporation, but the surface remained free of obvious salt precipitation. When the salt concentration was increased to 20 wt%, salt crystals began to appear on the surface and edges of C-LCDCA after 2 h of continuous evaporation, and large salt crystals appeared on the surface of the aerogel after 4 h.

As shown in Fig. 4h and Fig. S32, C-LCDCA4i enhanced its evaporation performance in pure water and salt solutions of different concentrations by side evaporation compared to C-LCDCA, and still achieved a good evaporation rate (1.64 kg m−2h−1) even in saturated salt solutions. The salt resistance of C-LCDCA4i was greatly improved compared with that of C-LCDCA as shown in Fig. 4i. No significant salt precipitation and stable evaporation rate were observed at WNaCl = 15 wt% after 8 h of continuous evaporation, and a few salt crystals were precipitated at the edge at WNaCl = 20 wt% for 8 h without significant decrease in evaporation rate. These results indicated that the evaporator still has excellent evaporation and salt-rejecting properties in salt solutions of different concentrations.

The best way to remove salt crystals precipitated during desalination is to use the self-cleaning properties of the material to re-dissolve the salt crystals back into the bulk water column. The C-LCDCA could re-dissolve the precipitated salt crystals (0.3 g) back into the bulk water, and the time to dissolve the salt crystals is affected by the concentration of the salt solution (Fig. S34). With excellent self-cleaning ability, C-LCDCA could dissolve 0.3 g NaCl salt crystals back into the water body in only 25 min in a salt solution with a concentration of less than 15 wt%. When the salt concentration increased to 20 wt%, the time for re-dissolution increased to 90 min, which reflected the salt precipitation of C-LCDCA in solutions with different salt concentrations, proving that the maximum tolerable salt concentration for long-term desalination was 20 wt%. The time taken for self-cleaning of C-LCDCA4i in 15 wt% and 20 wt% salt solutions was 40 % and 72 % less than that of C-LCDCA, respectively (Fig. S35). C-LCDCA4i still showed strong self-cleaning ability in 25 wt% saline solution, requiring 50 min to dissolve 0.3 g of NaCl salt crystals back into the bulk water. In addition to improving the salt resistance of the material, C-LCDCA4i also significantly reduced the time required to re-dissolve the salt crystals back into the bulk water and improved the self-cleaning ability of the material.

Precipitation and collection of salt crystals in special areas of the solar evaporator is an effective salt reduction strategy [47]. Interestingly, the collection of salt crystals could be achieved during the evaporation process. The salt crystals precipitated from the edges of C-LCDCA in WNaCl = 10 wt% (Video S1) and WNaCl = 15 wt% (Video S2) salt solutions by long-term evaporation of the material can be collected directly using physical means such as tweezers. The targeted collection of salt crystals can alleviate the degradation of evaporation performance caused by local crystallization phenomena, and the collected salt crystals can also be further processed and utilized to improve the practical application value of solar evaporators.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Efficient evaporation, salt-rejecting, and self-cleaning properties
ce:section.ce:para: A solar-powered evaporator with efficient evaporation, salt-rejecting, and self-cleaning performance was prepared based on the lignin carbon quantum dot modified chitosan carbon aerogel, as displayed in Fig. 5 . The solar evaporator achieved efficient evaporation through three main aspects: performance of photothermal conversion, water supply, and thermal management. The low-temperature carbonization and doping of LCQDs effectively enhanced the light absorption (Fig. 3f) and photothermal conversion (Fig. 3g) properties of the materials, ensuring that the evaporator could efficiently absorb sunlight and quickly convert it into heat energy to drive water evaporation. The low-temperature carbonization and doping of LCQDs ensured the hydrophilic properties (Fig. 3d and e) to provide adequate water supply for solar-powered evaporation. To improve the overall thermal management performance of the evaporator, water was supplied through paper belts imitating plant transpiration to avoid heat loss caused by direct contact between the evaporating material and the bulk water (Fig. 4a). Moreover, the evaporation performance of the evaporator was further enhanced by exploring the optimal number of layers of evaporation materials to integrate the photothermal conversion, water supply, and thermal management (Fig. 4d and Fig. S27).

In addition to efficient evaporation, salt-rejecting and self-cleaning performance are also important indicators for solar evaporators. Uniformly distributed LCQDs on chitosan aerogels had stronger localized heating resulting in faster gas–liquid exchange, salt exchange and balanced local salinity gradients driven by evaporation and temperature differences, and improved salt-rejecting of the evaporator (Fig. 5 and Fig. S23). The desalination ability of C-LCDCA was weakened at brine concentrations above 5 wt%, and the intensification of lateral evaporation resulted in the greatest evaporation rate at the upper surface edges of the material, which were the first to reach saturation and precipitate salt crystals (Fig. 4g). Imitation of plant transpiration for water supply led to the formation of a salinity gradient along the height of the evaporator and the highest salt concentration at the edges to achieve local crystallization to facilitate the removal of salt crystals by physical means. Super hydrophilic performance could quickly realize the salt exchange between the evaporator and water, break the salinity difference between the two, and realize self-cleaning. The excellent self-cleaning ability of the evaporator in water with different salinity levels proved its good salt resistance from the side (Fig. S34). The number of layers of the evaporator was adjusted to obtain the optimal height of the material, and the evaporation performance of the material was enhanced by lateral evaporation enhancement and water supply balancing (Fig. 4d). The increase in the height of the evaporator improved the overall salt resistance of the material, and the increase in the local salt concentration gradient and the salt concentration gradient along the height further improved the self-cleaning ability of the material (Fig. 4i and Fig. S35). Through the above process, the evaporator could achieve efficient evaporation, salt-resistance, and self-cleaning for salt solutions of different concentrations, thus realizing high-efficiency solar-powered desalination.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Purification of various water sources during evaporation
ce:section.ce:para: The purification ability of actual water such as seawater and industrial wastewater is one of the performance indicators of the evaporator. After solar desalination of the simulated seawater, four ions, including Na+, Mg2+, K+, and Ca2+, with a removal rate of well over 99 %, meeting the drinking water standards set by the WHO (Fig. 6 a and Table S8) [48,49]. To further investigate the ability of the evaporator to purify wastewater during solar water evaporation, a series of aqueous solutions containing dyes (RhB, MO, and MG), nanoplastics (500 and 50 nm), and heavy metal ions (Cr3+, Fe3+, Pb2+, and Ni2+) were used for evaporation experiments, respectively. The dyes and nanoplastics solutions were also effectively purified by solar water evaporation (Fig. 6b). The removal rates of both dyes and nanoplastics wastewater were close to 100 %. Moreover, Cr3+, Fe3+, Pb2+, and Ni2+, which are the most common heavy metal pollutants in industrial wastewater [50,51], were reduced from 10,000 ppm to less than 0.02 ppm respectively after solar water evaporation, with a removal rate close to 100 %, all of which met the heavy metal content standard in drinking water (GB5749-2006) set by the Chinese government (Fig. 6c) [20,48]. The above analysis indicates that the evaporator has good potential for desalination and purification of industrial wastewater containing dyes, nanoplastics, and heavy metal ions, as well as for drinking water production.

To further investigate the real solar desalination performance of the evaporator, outdoor evaporation experiments were conducted on C-LCDCA4i using a glass evaporation chamber in 3.5 wt% NaCl salt solution on the rooftop of Building 3, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Guangxi University (Nanning, China) from 9:00 to 17:00 on August 29, 2022, for 8 h, with outdoor temperature from 25 to 35 °C. The average light intensity of C-LCDCA4i during the 8 h outdoor evaporation was 0.80 kW m−2, the water evaporated with a total mass change of 16.31 kg m−2, and its average evaporation rate was 2.04 kg m−2h−1 (Fig. 6d). The highest evaporation rate of C-LCDCA4i was 2.64 kg m−2h−1 at 13:00 when it possessed the maximum value of light intensity (0.95 kW m−2) and outdoor temperature (35 °C). The evaporation rate of outdoor evaporation was slightly higher than the laboratory data due to the higher ambient outdoor temperature [52]. The above results further indicate that the aerogel evaporator has superb practical applications in solar water evaporation and desalination.

In this work, we have constructed a bionic solar-powered seawater evaporator based on the lignin carbon quantum dot modified chitosan carbon aerogel inspired by the transpiration of plants. Low-temperature carbonization enhanced the chitosan aerogel from matrix material to light-absorbing material. The introduction of LCQDs improved the hydrophilic properties and light absorption capacity of chitosan aerogel biochar. The resulting LCQDs modified chitosan carbon aerogel exhibited excellent evaporation rate and photothermal conversion efficiency. The C-LCDCA4i evaporator surpassed the limit of the interfacial evaporation system, with evaporation rate and efficiency up to 2.32 kg m−2h−1 and 113.9 % respectively under one sun. The evaporator offered excellent stability (strong acids and bases), salt resistance (different salt concentrations), and self-cleaning ability (re-dissolving salt crystals back into the bulk water). Moreover, the evaporator obtained condensate from simulated seawater that meets WHO standards for drinking water and achieved effective treatment of industrial wastewater containing heavy metal ions (Cr3+, Fe3+, Pb2+, and Ni2+), nanoplastics (500 and 50 nm), and dyes (RhB, MO, and MG). This work provided a new perspective for the preparation of high-performance and environmentally friendly solar evaporators with promising applications in seawater desalination, high-concentration brine treatment, and wastewater treatment containing multiple pollutants.

Zichen Li: Conceptualization, Investigation, Methodology, Writing – review & editing. Dazhen Li: Conceptualization, Investigation, Methodology, Writing – review & editing. Shuxia Wei: Methodology, Writing – review & editing. Xin Jin: Methodology, Writing – review & editing. Zheng Zhang: Methodology, Writing – review & editing. Lin Liu: Methodology, Writing – review & editing. Yuanyuan Ge: Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Zhili Li: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Methodology, Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21978057), Guangxi Natural Science Foundation of China (No. AD21220012), 100-Talent Program of Guangxi, China, Innovation Project of Guangxi Graduate Education (YCSW2023089), and the Students Innovation and Entrepreneurship Training Program of Guangxi University (S202210593142), China.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2024.150157.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary video 2
